summary:
The First Dynasty of Egypt, spanning approximately 3100 to 2900 BCE, was the initial ruling dynasty of the unified Egyptian state founded by [[event:1637|Narmer]], establishing the institutions, iconography, and administrative structures that would define pharaonic civilization for the next three thousand years. It comprised approximately eight kings — including Aha, Djer, Djet, Den, Anedjib, Semerkhet, and Qa'a — whose names and sequential order are known from king lists preserved in later monuments and from inscriptions found at royal tomb sites.

The First Dynasty kings ruled from [[event:1640|Memphis]], the newly founded capital at the junction of Upper and Lower Egypt, while building their tombs in the ancient sacred city of Abydos in Upper Egypt. The Abydos royal tombs grew increasingly elaborate over the dynasty's course, developing from mudbrick-lined pit graves into multi-chambered mortuary complexes with subsidiary burials of servants and retainers, and accompanying funerary enclosures (called valley enclosures or funerary palaces) in the nearby desert. The practice of human sacrifice to accompany royal burials — attested by dozens of subsidiary graves around the royal tombs — apparently ended by the close of the First Dynasty.

FirstDynasty Egypt witnessed the rapid expansion of the administrative apparatus of the state, the development of hieroglyphic writing into a more complete notational system capable of recording royal names and administrative data, and the elaboration of the dual-crown ideology representing the union of Upper and Lower Egypt. The serekh — the palace-facade frame enclosing the king's Horus name — became standardized as a royal symbol, and a range of royal titles and epithets were systematized. These conventions persisted with remarkably little change across three thousand years of subsequent Egyptian history.

Long-distance trade continued and expanded under First Dynasty rule. Egyptian expeditions to the Sinai exploited turquoise and copper mines, while relations with the Levant provided cedar timber and luxury goods. Increasingly sophisticated administrative documents — labels, seals, and tags — record the flow of commodities into and out of the royal household, providing the earliest evidence for Egypt's developing bureaucratic capacity. The First Dynasty thus represents not just the beginning of a dynasty but the founding moment of a state.
